annotations.text: The symptoms of a hydatid cyst are caused principally by the pressure of the expanding cyst, which is estimated to grow between 1 and 5 cm a year.
Fever, right-upper-quadrant abdominal pain, and malaise are the most prominent symptoms.
If the test is negative it should be repeated, and a specimen should be sent to the Centers for Disease Control for serologic confirmation.
Although I am troubled by the negative serologic test, there are certainly precedents for its occurrence.

The differential diagnosis of hepatic masses in children is broad. My task is to try to correlate the radiographic picture with the findings from the history, physical examination, and laboratory investigations.

Primary hepatic tumors are rare, but unfortunately nearly two thirds of them are malignant, representing the third most common abdominal cancer encountered in pediatric practice, after Wilms' tumor and neuroblastoma.
The finding of a large, solitary mass within the right lobe of the liver is typical of an amebic abscess and is present in the great majority of the cases.
The demonstration of a nonhomogeneous mass with septa by ultrasound and CT scans is nonspecific but certainly consistent with this diagnosis.
Caval compression by an amebic abscess has been described, even to the point of producing intraluminal thrombosis in the superior vena cava with extension into the right atrium.
The radiographic features of this case are also suggestive of this diagnosis.
This diagnosis seems to fit the overall clinical picture best.
The most probable clinical diagnosis in this case is an amebic abscess.

Since I cannot exclude the alternative diagnoses, such as a secondary infection of a hydatid cyst, a pyogenic liver abscess, or an infected hepatic tumor, I think that the surgeon should have proceeded with all these diagnoses in mind.
I think that the most likely diagnosis in this case is a primary amebic liver abscess.
The history of Mediterranean travel forces me to consider strongly a parasitic cause of the hepatic mass.
However, no single laboratory test is definitive, particularly if it is contrary to the clinical evidence.
